role_hints.oews_basis: 23-2093 Title Examiners, Abstractors, and Searchers 0.82% of the 4-digit 5411 wage bill — but 5411 is dominated by law offices (23-1011 Lawyers 62.15%), so the 4-digit mix badly understates this code's real structure; within 541191 proper, examiners/abstractors are plausibly ~20-25% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Automated search on digitized, indexed county records is far along; unindexed/historic records, metes-and-bounds legal descriptions and curative judgment still need humans. High within-role fraction where records are digital.

role_hints.oews_basis: not separately visible in the 5411 top-10 (escrow officers often coded under 13-2099/43-xxxx); hypothesis from industry knowledge
role_hints.note: CD/settlement-statement prep, disbursement checks and file coordination automate; licensed escrow duties, closing-table work and fraud-sensitive wire release remain human.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-xxxx office/admin occupations in the 5411 tail (43-9061 Office Clerks 1.00%, 43-6014 Secretaries 0.77% at 4-digit); hypothesis for this code
role_hints.note: Order entry, payoff requests, recording, policy issuance — workflow tasks AI handles well; the most compressible cost line.

role_hints.role: Owners / licensed title agents and managers
role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 2.29% at 4-digit plus owner comp largely outside wages; hypothesis
role_hints.note: Underwriter appointments, state licenses and realtor/lender relationships; AI replaces neither the license nor the referral relationship.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-xxxx sales occupations, not in the 5411 top-10; hypothesis
role_hints.note: Order flow is won through personal relationships with realtors, lenders and builders — the actual moat of a title agency.

source_hints.why: e-closing/RON/automation penetration among title and settlement agents — the adoption axis for this code.

source_hints.source: ALTA Title Insurance Market Share Data (from statutory Form 9 filings, quarterly)
source_hints.why: Premium volume and underwriter/agent splits — the regulated revenue structure that determines who keeps AI savings.

source_hints.source: State insurance department title rate filings (promulgated rates in TX/NM, filed rates elsewhere)
source_hints.why: Primary documents on whether premiums are regulator-set — a regulated price can either protect margin or force deflation via rate cases.

source_hints.source: FHFA/GSE title-acceptance pilots and attorney-opinion-letter (AOL) policy announcements
source_hints.why: The bypass channel: lender-side alternatives to full title insurance on low-risk refis.
Ongoing M&A coverage of agency acquisitions — consolidator counts and deal terms.

source_hints.source: FNF and First American investor disclosures on agency acquisitions
source_hints.why: The national underwriters are themselves the biggest consolidators; their filings show acquisition appetite and multiples paid.
Agency owner demographics and succession evidence; verify what ALTA currently publishes at agency level.

capture_questions: Title premiums are state-regulated (promulgated in Texas, filed elsewhere) and the agent keeps a filed premium split, while settlement/closing fees are separately priced per transaction: when AI cuts search and processing labor, does the regulated premium structure let the agency keep the spread as margin — or do rate regulators, underwriter split renegotiations and lender fee pressure claw it back? Split the answer by revenue line (premium split vs. settlement/escrow fees).

Who originates the order — realtor, lender, builder or attorney — and does the firm own any repeat relationship, or is flow captive to affiliated business arrangements (broker/lender JVs under RESPA Section 8 constraints)? What survives an acquisition?
Will GSE title-waiver and attorney-opinion-letter programs let lenders bypass full title search and settlement on low-risk transactions, and what share of current volume is exposed to that bypass?

Are the four national underwriters (FNF, First American, Old Republic, Stewart) expanding direct operations and squeezing independent agents' premium splits — i.e., is the upstream gatekeeper capturing the AI margin before the agency sees it?
terminal_value_question: If AI title search on digitized records matures and GSEs accept alternative title assurance, the labor content of a closing collapses — does paid demand persist because state-regulated title insurance premiums and lender requirements are a legal toll independent of labor content (protecting agency economics), or does the product itself shrink to insurance distribution plus curative work on hard titles, with regulators cutting promulgated rates once cost structures visibly drop? Distinguish AI cutting the agency's cost from AI (plus GSE policy) removing the reason the agency is paid.
special_notes: Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5411, dominated by law-office occupations (Lawyers 62.15%) — do not use it as-is; 23-2093 Title Examiners at 0.82% is this code's signature occupation leaking into the parent. Build the V breakdown from the hypothesis structure and verify against ALTA operations data.

Pricing-model nuance: premium-split revenue is rate-regulated (deflation only via rate cases — slow), while settlement/escrow fees are market-priced (deflation via lender/realtor pressure — fast). Model pi_dist separately for the two lines.

Order flow is controlled upstream by realtors and lenders — the firm often does not own its demand; affiliated business arrangements (RESPA-constrained JVs) are the institutionalized form of that capture. This is the central C-factor trap.
Extreme mortgage-cycle sensitivity: 2021-2022 refi-boom financials are not representative; normalize any revenue or margin year against MBA origination volume.

Boundary confusion: attorney-closing states (GA, SC, MA, NY and others) shift settlement work into 541110 law offices; title insurance underwriters themselves are NAICS 524127 — keep agency (this code) separate from underwriter economics in comps and margins.
Entry barriers are underwriter appointment and state title/escrow licensure, not skill — a moat for incumbents but one that platforms and underwriter direct operations can route around.
